title: Exploring and evaluating personalized models for code generation

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## EXPLORING AND EVALUATING PERSONALIZED MODELS FOR CODE GENERATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Large Transformer models achieved the state-of-the-art status for Natural Language Understanding and are increasingly the baseline architecture for source code generation models. Transformers are usually pre-trained on a large unsupervised corpus, learning token representations and transformations relevant to modeling generally available text, and then fine-tuned on a particular task of interest. While fine-tuning is a tried-and-true method for adapting a model to a new domain, for example question-answering on a given topic or a source code generation model, generalization remains an on-going challenge. Here we explore the ability of various levels of model fine-tuning to improve generalization by personalized fine-tuning. In the context of generating unit tests for Java methods, here we evaluate learning to personalize to a specific project using several methods to personalize transformer models for unit test generation for a specific Java project. We consider three fine-tuning approaches: (i) _custom_ fine-tuning, which allows all the model parameters to be tuned; (ii) _lightweight_ fine-tuning, which freezes most of the model’s parameters, allowing a tuning of the token embeddings and softmax layer or the final layer alone; (iii) _prefix_ tuning, which keeps language model parameters frozen, but optimizes a small project-specific prefix vector. Each of these techniques offers a different trade-off in total compute cost and prediction performance, which we evaluate by code and task-specific metrics, training time, and total computational operations. We compare these fine-tuning strategies for code generation and discuss the potential generalization and cost benefits of each in deployment scenarios.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

It is well-known that even the best models can fail to generalize properly to new domains, and even to new users of said models. For example, a model trained to answer questions in general may not answer StackOverflow questions as well as the questions in the training domain, or a software developer in an Enterprise environment with private code may have libraries and attribute name which differ from public source code used to train a code synthesis model.

The current dominant paradigm in Natural Language Processing (NLP) modeling is to pre-train a large transformer model (Vaswani et al., 2017a) on a large corpus and then fine-tune it on a particular task of interest. For example, a question-answering (Q&A) model is generally first pre-trained on a large corpus of textual data for the specific language ( _e.g.,_ Wikipedia, and news articles in English), then fine-tuned on a task-specific dataset of paired questions and corresponding answers. The pre-training process aims at learning semantic vector representation of the language and words, while the fine-tuning process specializes the model for a specific domain.

Transformer models are also increasingly the baseline architecture used for code generation tasks, such as writing methods from natural language description (Clement et al., 2020; Austin et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021), or generating test cases from the focal method under test (Tufano et al., 2021). Similarly for NLP tasks these models are pre-trained on a large corpus of natural text and publicly available source code and then fine-tuned on a specific code-related task. Further, these models also may not generalize to new domains of interest, and can benefit from task or even user-specific fine-tuning, here called customization or personalization. Customization is particularly relevant for code generation models since it provides several benefits:
- the opportunity to improve data privacy by considering private or sensitive data only during the customization process on the client side;
- the opportunity to reduce deployment cost as customized models can offer better user performance without increasing model size.

Custom models can provide clear benefits to users and model providers. We envision serving tens or hundreds of thousands of custom models, but doing so presents several logistical hurdles, including the costs of training, storing, and loading these models into GPU memory for inference. Worse, memory costs will only be exacerbated when working with ever larger and more powerful models.

For these reasons, we investigate several customization approaches, some of which can dramatically reduce the memory footprint and amortized computational cost introduced by custom models. Specifically, we consider three fine-tuning approaches: (i) _custom_ fine-tuning, which allows all the model parameters to be tuned; (ii) _lightweight_ fine-tuning, which only optimizes the token embedding representations or the final softmax layer; (iii) _prefix_ tuning, which keeps language model parameters frozen, but optimizes a small project-specific vector prefix.

In our extensive empirical evaluation we found that all the customization strategies lead to significant model improvements on a target project in terms of both intrinsic and task-specific metrics. While there is no unambiguous winner among the customization strategies, each approach can provide specific benefits in particular deployment scenarios. This paper provides insights on these customization strategies, their benefits and drawbacks, as well as providing guidelines and suggestions on which one to use based on the training cost, memory and storage, number of users, and deployment scenarios.

## 2 APPROACH

This section describes the proposed customization approach for code generation models. We begin by formally defining the customization process, then we provide details for each of the fine-tuning strategies.

## 2.1 CUSTOMIZATION PROCESS

We use the term _customization_ to refer to the process of fine-tuning a model _m_ , previously trained on a generic dataset for a task _t_ , with the goal of improving its performance on a specific dataset _p_ . The performance of a machine learning model _m_ on a dataset _p_ is measured by one or more evaluation functions _f_ ( _m, p_ ), where _f_ can be either a maximization ( _e.g.,_ BLEU, top-k accuracy) or minimization ( _e.g.,_ perplexity) function. The customization process is designed to modify the trainable parameters of the model _m_ , obtaining the model _m[′]_ , such that the performance of _m[′]_ on _p_ is better than what was attained by _m_ . Specifically, _f_ ( _m[′] , p_ ) _> f_ ( _m, p_ ) for maximization functions, or _f_ ( _m[′] , p_ ) _< f_ ( _m, p_ ) for minimization functions.
In this work, _m_ is an encoder-decoder transformer model, _t_ is a code generation task, and _p_ is a target software project to which we intend to customize _m_ .

## 2.2 CUSTOM FINE-TUNING

Custom fine-tuning is the most straightforward customization approach. The model to be customized is taken as is and trained on a selected project. All parameters are trainable during this process. Figure 1a shows the model during fine-tuning, where all the parameters from the encoder and decoder blocks, as well as embeddings and output layers can be modified.

## 2.3 LIGHTWEIGHT FINE-TUNING - EMBEDDINGS AND OUTPUT LAYER (L-EO)

Fully fine-tuning a model for every project or user may be prohibitive in terms of storage and memory costs. As a result, we explore ways to mitigate these costs by reducing the number of parameters that vary from one custom model to another. In our lightweight fine-tuning experiments, we achieve this

Figure 1b shows the Lightweight fine-tuning - Embeddings and Output Layer (L-EO) design, where most of the model parameters are frozen (displayed in gray), and we allow only the embedding and output layers parameters to be fine-tuned, following the approach in Lu et al. (2021).

## 2.4 LIGHTWEIGHT FINE-TUNING - LAST DECODER BLOCK (L-LDB)

In this lightweight fine-tuning strategy, shown in Figure 1c (L-LDB), most of the model’s parameters are kept frozen, while only the parameters in the last decoder block are trainable, this includes: self-attention, encoder-decoder attention, layernorm and feedforward layers. This design decision of training only the last decoder block is motivated by experimental results analyzing the model’s parameter changes during custom fine-tuning. Figure 2 reports the average absolute changes, during fine-tuning, in the parameters belonging to different Encoder and Decoder blocks for a BART model. We observe that, as we go through the transformer model, the average change in parameter values tends to increase, with the last decoder block showing the highest changes in parameter values. As a result, we hypothesize that it could be sufficient to tune the last decoder block and obtain performance improvements similar to the fully custom fine-tuned model.

## 2.5 PREFIX TUNING

Prefix tuning was first introduced by Li and Liang (2021), with the goal of fine-tuning a general model for different tasks. The technique concatenates a sequence (prefix) of virtual tokens (trainable parameters) to the front of the input of every encoder and decoder block. In our context, the intuition behind this approach is that the prefix embeds the properties of a specific project, which allows the model to generate customized responses for that repository. Practically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of 1024 _×_ 200 _×_ 24 _×_ 2 _≈_ 10M trainable parameters. The prefix is initialized to the most frequent words in the repository for which the model is customized.

## 3 EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN

The goal of our experimental design is to investigate whether custom models outperform the baseline model, leading to performance improvements in terms of intrinsic metrics (RQ1), as well as extrinsic task-specific metrics (RQ2). Next, we analyze and compare the different customization approaches in terms of training and compute costs (RQ3) as well as model size and required storage for deployment.

In our case study, we chose Unit Test Case generation as our code generation task _t_ , and _AthenaTest_ by Tufano et al. (2021) as our baseline model _m_ , which is a BART transformer model pre-trained on source code and English, and fine-tuned on Java unit test generation. The task is modeled as a translation task, where the input is a focal method ( _i.e.,_ method under test), and the output is a test case which tests the focal method’s correctness. We randomly sample 20 projects from the test set, each of those representing the dataset _p_ on which a custom model is fine-tuned. Specifically, for each project _p_ , we start from the baseline model _m_ and fine-tune four different custom models according to the four proposed fine-tuning strategies. For each project and fine-tuning strategy ( _e.g.,_ L-EO), we fine-tune and evaluate the models using 4-fold cross-validation. The models are trained until the best validation loss is reached, independently for every fold, every repository, and every customization approach. In total, we fine-tune and evaluate 20( _projects_ ) _×_ 4( _approaches_ ) _×_ 4( _folds_ ) = 320 models.

## 3.1 DATASET

Table 2 reports information about the 20 GitHub repositories sampled from the test set, which will be used to customize our models. The table shows (i) the Project ID, which will be used in the paper to

The list of projects represent a diverse set of repositories with different size, domain, and popularity. They span from small personal projects ( _e.g.,_ Tutorials with 6 stars), to open source projects developed by large organizations such as Apache and Google.

## 3.2 RQ1: INTRINSIC EVALUATION METRICS

**RQ** 1 **: Do custom models obtain better performances on intrinsic metrics, such as BLEU and perplexity, w.r.t. the baseline?** To begin, we investigate how the different model customization approaches described in Sec. 2 score on intrinsic metrics such as BLEU and perplexity. All approaches entail fine-tuning the baseline model to the dataset of a specific project, with the choice of parameters being tuned depending on the approach taken. The four variants are trained independently until the best validation loss is achieved. We report the BLEU4 score and the mean perplexity per token on the test fold, for all the 20 projects. Next, we perform statistical tests to investigate whether the observed differences between the baseline and custom models are significant, as well as differences among the customization approaches. Specifically, we rely on the Kruskal-Wallis test, a non-parametric statistical test.

- 3.3 RQ2: TASK-SPECIFIC PERFORMANCES

**RQ** 2 **: Do custom models improve on performance metrics specific to unit test generation?** We want to investigate how the different customization approaches compare with respect to the downstream task of generating unit tests. Beyond BLEU score and perplexity, we would like to see if custom models can produce the correct target code, how closely their unit tests mimic the repository style, or even if they can perfectly match the desired output.

- _Perfect Matches_ : We compare the model’s output string with the target developer-written unit test. If the two strings are identical, this is considered a perfect match. We do not take spacing and indentation into account as we are using a Java dataset (where indentation is not required). We report the proportion of perfect matches among the top 5 model predictions.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

- _Abstracted Code Matches_ : We pass the model output and target output through the src2abs tool (Tufano, 2018), to obtain an abstracted version, masking variable names, method names, etc. We also do not distinguish between different objects of the same type.

- _Coding Style_ : For each project’s custom model, we would like to determine how closely the model learns the developer’s personal programming style and preferences. To this end, we extract the collection of all identifiers ( _i.e.,_ variables and functions’ names) from the unit tests written by the developer as well as those generated by the models. We then pass these text outputs through a tf-idf vectorizer and compute the cosine similarity between them. This allows us to compare the developer’s and the models’ word usage. We examine the similarity between the developer’s unit tests and the baseline and custom models generated tests. This scores the vocabulary similarity of the unit tests with the model generated code.

- 3.4 RQ3: TRAINING COST COMPARISON

**RQ** 3 **: Given the same amount of compute, which custom models achieve the biggest performance improvement?** Since our four training regimes tune a different number of parameters, simply comparing the training time or number of optimization steps to reach the best validation loss may not be appropriate. For a model with _N_ parameters, we approximate the computation cost of a forward pass to be _C ≈_ 2 _N_ floating point operations per training token, with an additional correction for embedding layers. The backward pass takes roughly twice the amount of compute, but it is unnecessary for layers that are frozen. For additional details, we refer to Table 1 in Kaplan et al. (2020). We report the resulting compute in petaFLOPS-seconds.

## 4 RESULTS

## 4.1 RQ1: INTRINSIC EVALUATION METRICS

Table 3 presents the results of custom models in terms of the intrinsic metrics: BLEU and perplexity. Specifically, for each project, we report the average BLEU and perplexity over the four folds, achieved on the test set by the different customization strategy, as well as the baseline model. We observe notable improvements in both metrics for every project w.r.t. the baseline, with BLEU going from 16.1 achieved by the baseline model to 36-38 by custom models.

The statistical tests reported in Table 4 confirm that the improvement observed by the four customization techniques are statistically significant w.r.t. the baseline ( _p <_ 1e-7). However, we do not observe statistical significance in the differences among the customization strategies, meaning that, in terms of intrinsic metrics performances, the differences are within margin of error.

## 4.2 RQ2: TASK-SPECIFIC PERFORMANCES

The results in terms of task-specific performances are presented in Figure 3. The plot 3a shows the top-k accuracy for perfect matches (solid line) and abstracted matches (dotted line), aggregated over the 20 projects. The baseline model outputs the same code structure (abstracted) in roughly 3% of all cases, and virtually never produces the exact target output (<1%). Moreover, its performance does not improve as we consider more predictions. All customization processes show significant improvement compared to the baseline. Specifically, these improvements are observed for every single project (full results will be available on our online appendix). Customized models produce the correct code structure as their top prediction in _∼_ 13-14% of instances, and a perfect match in _∼_ 4-6% of cases. They also tend to improve as we consider their top 5 predictions. Between the different customization processes, Custom consistently performs the best, closely followed by Prefix and L-LDB. When considering abstracted code matches, these three approaches are nearly identical. L-EO, however, performs slightly worse than the others.

Plot 3b shows the distribution of tf-idf cosine similarity computed between identifiers used in the developers’ written tests and the models’ generated outputs. We observe that the distribution for custom models is skewed towards the higher values of cosine similarity. This result demonstrates that custom models tend to use variable and function names that are more similar to what developers used in their own tests.

## 4.3 RQ3: TRAINING COST COMPARISON

For each customization process, we plot validation loss as a function of compute, as defined in section 3.4. The results are presented in Figure 4, where the light lines represent the validation loss curve for each individual project and fold, while the bold line represents the average for each custom strategy. First note that Custom achieves very large gains during the first epoch, as evidenced by the fact that its validation loss starts much lower than L-EO and L-LDB. Custom also outperforms other customization processes when given a limited amount of compute. However, we observe that beyond a certain amount of compute, Custom and L-LDB tend to achieve similar performances. In contrast, L-EO starts at the same validation loss as L-LDB but converges much slower to the best loss, requiring 2-3 times as much compute.

Since the prefix parameters suffer from poor initialization, Prefix is the most expensive customization process. To overcome this problem, it is possible to first train the prefix on a large generic dataset. Then, given proper hyperparameter tuning, it is possible to substantially cut down compute cost for customizing the prefix.

## 5 DISCUSSION & LESSONS LEARNED

The four customization strategies considered in this work are effective in improving a code generation model’s performances on a given software project. Specifically, all custom models significantly outperform the baseline in terms of intrinsic metrics ( _i.e.,_ BLEU and perplexity) as well as taskspecific metrics ( _i.e.,_ abstract and raw matches). While the differences among the customization approaches are not significant (no clear winner), each strategy offers specific advantages in different circumstances and deployment scenarios.

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

_**Custom**_ fine-tuning achieves the overall best performances and the customization process is relatively fast and efficient. This is somewhat expected, since this customization strategy allows all the model’s parameters to be tuned on the specific project. This characteristic also leads to the major disadvantage of this approach: each custom model is an entire copy of the original model. Storage and inference costs could become prohibitive when serving many users with personalized custom models.

_**Lightweight**_ fine-tuning achieves good results while training fewer parameters. This allows to serve potentially many users with custom models which can be stored and loaded efficiently. Specifically, L-LDB trains fewer parameters than L-EO, however the latter could allow to deploy the embedding and output layers on the user side, with a privacy-preserving focus.

_**Prefix**_ fine-tuning trains the lowest number of parameters (only 2.4% for a BART model), while improving over the baseline. However, it increases the total number of parameters of the model (prefixes are additional virtual tokens) and requires more compute time to achieve good performances, mostly due to the prefix initialization problem. On the bright side, this strategy allows to batch together requests from different users (with different prefixes), which can be processed by a single model, generating personalized outputs.

## 6 RELATED WORK

This work is related to two areas of the existing literature: neural source code generation and model personalization. Neural code generation has generated an intense recent interest in NLP, using Transformer models Vaswani et al. (2017b) in particular for code completion Svyatkovskiy et al. (2020; 2019); Clement et al. (2020); Raychev et al. (2014); Bruch et al. (2009); Brockschmidt et al. (2018), code synthesis from examples Chen et al. (2018), natural language to code Clement et al. (2020); Chen et al. (2018); Austin et al. (2021), code feature summarization Liu et al. (2021); Moreno et al. (2013); Scalabrino et al. (2017); Wan et al. (2018); Alon et al. (2018); Moreno et al. (2014), code search Husain et al. (2019); Feng et al. (2020), unit test generation Tufano et al. (2021) and even bug fixing Drain et al. (2021) and detection Zhai et al. (2020). This paper naturally is an extension and evaluation of personalized unit test generations as studied by Tufano et al. (2021), and an important contribution to the understanding optimization in a deployment scenario.

Much of the previous literature on personalized models focuses on client-side training to keep data on device Shor et al. (2019); Popov et al. (2018), and most work is in the domain of search query completion Jaech and Ostendorf (2018), natural language completion Popov et al. (2018), or even automated speech recognition Shor et al. (2019). Naturally this work extends the domain of evaluation beyond natural language tasks and into the software engineering domain. This paper does not evaluate methods for client side training with restricted resources, however, as the most powerful large language models which enable state of the art code synthesis have 10-100 million parameters. At the time of writing such large models cannot be executed in a reasonable amount of time on most consumer laptops. We leave to future work extending these studies to models which have been pruned, quantized, distilled, and optimized to be ran in limited resource environments.

## 7 CONCLUSION

In this paper we explored different ways to customize a code generation model for a given codebase, with the goal of improving its performances on a target project. We described and analyzed four customization strategies and applied them on 20 different software projects for the task of generating unit test cases. Specifically, we considered the following strategies: (i) _custom_ fine-tuning, which allows all the model parameters to be tuned on the target project; (ii) _L-EO_ fine-tuning, a lightweight training which freezes most of the model’s parameters, tuning only embedding and output layers; (iii) _L-LDB_ fine-tuning, a lightweight training which only tunes the last decoder block; (iv) _prefix_ tuning, which keeps language model parameters frozen, but optimizes a small project-specific vector (prefix).

In our extensive empirical evaluation we found that all the customization strategies lead to significant model’s improvements on a target project, in terms of both intrinsic and task-specific metrics, with the custom models adapting to the coding style of the target project. While there is no clear winner among the customization strategies, each approach can provide specific benefits in particular deployment scenarios.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper addresses a timely and practical problem—personalizing code generation models for specific projects—and evaluates multiple parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategies with a clear empirical setup. It provides useful insights into trade-offs between performance, cost, and model size, and its focus on deployment considerations (e.g., storage, compute) adds practical value. The experimental design is generally sound, with a diverse set of projects and well-defined metrics.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient ablation across personalization strategies under matched conditions: The paper compares four personalization methods but does not ensure they are evaluated under comparable optimization budgets or convergence criteria. For example, Figure 4 shows that prefix tuning requires more compute to converge due to poor initialization, yet the final performance comparison in Table 3 and Figure 3 does not account for this difference in training dynamics. Without normalizing for compute or showing performance at equal FLOP budgets, the comparison risks favoring methods that simply train longer. A fairer evaluation would report performance at fixed compute levels or include a warm-started prefix tuning variant to isolate the impact of initialization from the method itself.

- Risk of data leakage in project-specific fine-tuning: The customization process fine-tunes on data from a target project, but the paper does not clarify whether the training and test sets are properly separated at the method level. Since unit test generation involves generating tests for specific focal methods, if any focal method or its variants appear in both training and test folds, this would lead to direct data leakage. Given that the dataset is drawn from real GitHub repositories, such overlap is plausible. The authors must explicitly confirm that test methods were excluded from training and describe how code deduplication was handled to ensure valid generalization claims.

- Overreliance on string-based and lexical metrics for functional tasks: The primary extrinsic metrics—perfect match and abstracted match—rely on string or AST-level similarity rather than functional correctness. A generated test may match the target string but fail to compile or pass, or it may differ lexically while being functionally equivalent. The absence of execution-based evaluation (e.g., test compilation, passing rate on the focal method) undermines the validity of the task-specific claims. For a unit test generation task, functional correctness should be a core metric, not an optional add-on.

- Unclear definition and sampling of 'project-specific' data: The paper treats each GitHub project as a personalization domain but does not specify how data points (focal method–test pairs) are sampled per project. Are they uniformly sampled? Are they filtered by activity, authorship, or test quality? Without this information, it is unclear whether the personalization effect stems from learning a consistent coding style or simply memorizing frequent patterns. Moreover, the assumption that a project reflects a single 'style' is questionable for large, multi-author repositories like Apache Dubbo. The paper should clarify data selection criteria and consider author-level personalization as a more coherent unit.

- Missing analysis of personalization vs. domain adaptation: The paper assumes that improvements stem from capturing project-specific style, but it does not disentangle this from general domain adaptation effects. For instance, fine-tuning on any Java code from the target project—regardless of authorship—might improve performance due to library usage, API patterns, or project-specific conventions. The current design cannot distinguish whether gains are due to personalization (i.e., modeling individual or team style) or simply better domain alignment. A control experiment using held-out projects with similar domains but different styles would strengthen this claim.

- Evaluation limited to one task and language: All experiments focus on unit test generation for Java, which limits the generalizability of the findings. Personalization effects may differ for other tasks (e.g., code completion, bug fixing) or languages (e.g., Python, JavaScript) with different syntactic and idiomatic conventions. Given recent work on multi-language and multi-task code models, the paper should either justify Java/unit test focus as representative or acknowledge the limitation and call for broader evaluation in future work.

- No evaluation of functional correctness for generated tests: The paper measures task performance using perfect string matches and abstracted AST matches, but does not test whether generated unit tests compile or pass when run against the focal method. For a task called unit test generation, this is a core omission. A generated test might match the target syntactically but fail to execute, or differ slightly in syntax while being functionally correct. Readers will wonder whether the customization actually produces working code. To fix this, the authors should report the test passing rate—i.e., the fraction of generated tests that successfully compile and pass—on a sample of 5–10 projects, using a standard Java testing framework like JUnit. This would ground the claims in real utility.

- Missing comparison to domain adaptation baselines: The paper attributes performance gains to personalization, but does not rule out that improvements come from general domain adaptation—such as learning project-specific libraries or API usage—rather than coding style. A natural question is whether fine-tuning on any in-domain code (e.g., non-test code from the same project) yields similar gains. To isolate the personalization effect, the authors should add a baseline that fine-tunes on production code from the target project (excluding test files) and compare its performance to the test-data-finetuned models. Without this, the claim that models are capturing 'coding style' is speculative.

- No analysis of personalization at the developer level: The paper treats each GitHub project as a single personalization unit, but many projects in Table 2 (e.g., Apache Dubbo, Drools) are large, multi-author codebases. It is unclear how a single model can capture a coherent 'style' in such cases. The analysis would benefit from a breakdown by author: for projects with multiple major contributors, the authors should measure whether model performance correlates with author-specific patterns (e.g., generating better tests for methods written by frequent authors). This could be done by computing identifier similarity or match rates per author. Otherwise, the notion of 'project-level personalization' risks being ill-defined.

- Lack of deployment cost simulation for multi-user scenarios: A key claim is that lightweight methods (e.g., prefix tuning) enable efficient deployment for many users, but the paper provides no quantitative estimate of memory or latency under realistic serving conditions. For example, how many prefix-tuned models can be stored in GPU memory simultaneously? How does batching across prefixes affect inference speed? The authors should simulate a deployment scenario: given a 40GB GPU, compute how many custom models can be hosted using each method (custom, L-LDB, prefix), and estimate the cost per inference. This would make the deployment guidelines concrete rather than qualitative.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a controlled ablation study comparing personalization methods at matched compute budgets, including a warm-started prefix tuning variant

- Explicitly describe the train/test split procedure and confirm no overlap at the method level to rule out data leakage

- Include functional evaluation metrics such as test compilation rate or passing rate on the focal method

- Clarify how data points are sampled per project and justify project-level personalization over author-level

- Discuss whether observed gains are due to personalization or general domain adaptation, possibly adding a cross-project control analysis

comments.title: Sampling projects from test set risks data leakage
comments.quote: We randomly sample 20 projects from the test set, each of those representing the dataset  $p$  on which a custom model is fine-tuned.
comments.explanation: The paper states that 20 projects were sampled from the test set and then used for model customization—a critical flaw because it implies test data is being used to train or adapt models. This creates direct data leakage: if any focal method or test case in the test set is later used for evaluation, the model may have already seen it during fine-tuning. The authors likely meant to sample from the full dataset before any train/test split. Clarify that the 20 projects were selected from the full corpus, with test data strictly held out for evaluation, to ensure valid generalization claims.

comments.title: Perfect match metric ignores functional correctness
comments.quote: We compare the model's output string with the target developer-written unit test. If the two strings are identical, this is considered a perfect match. We do not take spacing and indentation into account as we are using a Java dataset (where indentation is not required). We report the proportion of perfect matches among the top 5 model predictions.
comments.explanation: The perfect match criterion requires exact string identity (ignoring only whitespace), which fails to account for functional equivalence. A generated test may differ in variable names or assertion order but still compile and pass, while a verbatim copy may fail due to missing imports or broken references. The real task is generating working tests, not reproducing strings. Without execution-based validation, the metric misaligns with the downstream objective. Report the test passing rate—i.e., the fraction of generated tests that compile and pass—on a sample of projects using a standard framework like JUnit, to ground performance in actual utility.

comments.title: Style metric conflates project conventions with individual developer style
comments.quote: For each project’s custom model, we would like to determine how closely the model learns the developer’s personal programming style and preferences. To this end, we extract the collection of all identifiers (i.e., variables and functions’ names) from the unit tests written by the developer as well as those generated by the models. We then pass these text outputs through a tf-idf vectorizer and compute the cosine similarity between them.
comments.explanation: The claim is that models learn 'the developer’s personal programming style', but the method aggregates identifiers across all unit tests in a project without author attribution. In multi-developer repositories like Apache Dubbo, the 'developer-written' tests are a mix of contributions. The tf-idf similarity reflects convergence to project-level naming patterns, not individual idiosyncrasies. For example, if multiple developers use 'builder' patterns, the model learns the project norm, not personal style. This undermines the interpretation of personalization. Revise the claim to reflect project-level adaptation and consider author-level analysis in future work.

comments.title: Unclear stopping criterion across strategies
comments.quote: The models are trained until the best validation loss is reached, independently for every fold, every repository, and every customization approach.
comments.explanation: Training each model to its own best validation loss assumes comparable convergence behavior across strategies, but prefix tuning is known to converge slowly due to poor initialization. This means lightweight methods may stop early not because they’ve converged, but because they haven’t yet improved on the validation set—potentially underestimating their performance. The paper should either report convergence curves or justify that all models reached a stable minimum within a consistent number of steps. Otherwise, the comparison risks favoring faster-converging methods like full fine-tuning.

comments.title: Backward pass FLOP count overestimated
comments.quote: For a model with $N$ parameters, we approximate the computation cost of a forward pass to be $C\approx 2N$ floating point operations per training token, with an additional correction for embedding layers. The backward pass takes roughly twice the amount of compute, but it is unnecessary for layers that are frozen.
comments.explanation: The claim that the backward pass takes 'roughly twice the amount of compute' as the forward pass is incorrect. In standard deep learning models, the backward pass requires approximately the same number of floating point operations as the forward pass due to the chain rule. If the forward pass is ~2N FLOPs, the backward pass is also ~2N, not ~4N. While frozen layers reduce backward cost, the base multiplier is wrong. This could lead to systematic overestimation of training costs, especially for full fine-tuning. Correct the statement to reflect that the backward pass requires roughly the same compute as the forward pass.

comments.title: Incorrect p-value interpretation in Table 4
comments.quote:
|  Cust. | 3e-08 | - | 0.4 | 0.7 | 0.7 | 3e-08 | - | 0.5 | 0.9 | 0.9  |
|  EO | 3e-08 | 0.4 | - | 0.5 | 0.7 | 3e-08 | 0.5 | - | 0.5 | 0.5  |
|  LDB | 3e-08 | 0.7 | 0.5 | - | 0.9 | 3e-08 | 0.9 | 0.5 | - | 0.8  |
|  Prefix | 3e-08 | 0.7 | 0.7 | 0.9 | - | 3e-08 | 0.9 | 0.5 | 0.8 | -  |

Table 4: Kruskal-Wallis Test p-values testing the significance of the pairwise hypothesis that one customization method is superior than another. Custom strategies are significantly better than baseline.
comments.explanation: The Kruskal-Wallis test is an omnibus test and does not produce pairwise p-values. The values in Table 4 (e.g., 0.4, 0.7) must come from a post-hoc test like Dunn’s, not Kruskal-Wallis itself. Labeling them as 'Kruskal-Wallis Test p-values' is statistically incorrect. Furthermore, the high p-values between methods (e.g., 0.7 between Custom and Prefix) indicate no significant difference, contradicting the narrative that Custom 'consistently performs the best'. Revise the caption to accurately describe the statistical procedure and avoid overinterpreting non-significant pairwise comparisons.

comments.title: Inconsistent labeling in Table 4
comments.explanation: The row labels use 'EO' and 'LDB', while the column headers use 'L-EO' and 'L-LDB'. This inconsistency introduces ambiguity: it is not formally established whether 'EO' refers to 'L-EO' or a different variant. Although context suggests equivalence, formal clarity requires exact naming. Change the row labels to 'L-EO' and 'L-LDB' to match the columns, ensuring full consistency and preventing misinterpretation of the pairwise comparisons.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about prefix tuning initialization
comments.quote: Since the prefix parameters suffer from poor initialization, Prefix is the most expensive customization process. To overcome this problem, it is possible to first train the prefix on a large generic dataset. Then, given proper hyperparameter tuning, it is possible to substantially cut down compute cost for customizing the prefix.
comments.explanation: The paper attributes prefix tuning’s high cost solely to poor initialization, but no ablation study supports this. There is no comparison between different initialization schemes or analysis of gradient dynamics. Alternative factors—such as the quadratic cost of extended attention or low-rank adaptation limits—are not ruled out. Moreover, the proposed solution (pre-training on generic data) is hypothetical and not evaluated. This overstates the findings. Rephrase to acknowledge that slow convergence may stem from multiple factors, including architecture and initialization, and avoid presenting untested solutions as established remedies.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of customization strategy outcomes
comments.quote: While there is no clear winner among the customization strategies, each approach can provide specific benefits in particular deployment scenarios.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'no clear winner' contradicts the paper’s own results. Section 4.2 shows that full fine-tuning consistently outperforms lightweight methods in task-specific metrics, and Figure 3 indicates a performance hierarchy. While trade-offs exist in cost and efficiency, the data support a performance ranking. Presenting all methods as equally viable overgeneralizes the findings. Revise to acknowledge that full fine-tuning achieves the highest performance, while lightweight methods offer competitive results with lower resource demands, making them preferable in constrained settings.

comments.title: Overstatement of coding style adaptation
comments.quote: in terms of both intrinsic and task-specific metrics, with the custom models adapting to the coding style of the target project.
comments.explanation: The claim that models adapt to 'coding style' goes beyond the evidence. The paper only measures identifier similarity via tf-idf, which captures lexical reuse of variable and function names. 'Coding style' includes broader aspects like control flow, API usage, test structure, and error handling—none of which are analyzed. Without evidence beyond naming patterns, the conclusion overreaches. Revise to state that models generate identifiers more similar to those in the target project, avoiding implications of holistic style adaptation.

comments.title: Ambiguous sampling of project datasets
comments.quote: We randomly sample 20 projects from the test set, each of those representing the dataset  $p$  on which a custom model is fine-tuned.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'randomly sample 20 projects from the test set' is ambiguous and potentially misleading. If the test set is used for final evaluation, sampling projects from it for customization violates standard protocol. The authors likely meant to sample from the full dataset and then split each into train/validation/test. Clarify that the 20 projects were selected from the full corpus prior to any data splitting, and confirm that no overlap exists between customization (training) and evaluation (test) data at the method level.

comments.title: Missing justification for prefix length choice
comments.quote: Practically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of  $1024 \times 200 \times 24 \times 2 \approx 10\mathrm{M}$  trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: The choice of 200 tokens for the prefix length is presented as practical but lacks empirical or theoretical justification. No ablation study or reference supports why 200 is optimal. Shorter prefixes may suffice, or longer ones may be needed for complex repositories. Add a rationale—e.g., 'This length was selected based on preliminary experiments showing saturation in performance beyond 150 tokens, consistent with findings in Li and Liang (2021)'—to strengthen reproducibility and design transparency.

comments.title: Unverified layer count in parameter calculation
comments.quote: Practically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of  $1024 \times 200 \times 24 \times 2 \approx 10\mathrm{M}$  trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: The parameter count assumes 24 transformer layers and prefixes on both encoder and decoder, but the model architecture is not specified. For a T5-Base model (12 layers), this would yield ~4.9M parameters, not 10M. Only T5-XXL (24 layers) matches the stated count. Without confirming the model variant, the factor of 24 lacks support. Specify the model architecture or revise the formula to include the layer count as a variable (e.g., $1024 \times 200 \times L \times 2$) to ensure accuracy.

comments.title: Ambiguous initialization of virtual tokens
comments.quote: The prefix is initialized to the most frequent words in the repository for which the model is customized.
comments.explanation: Virtual tokens are continuous embeddings and cannot be initialized 'to' discrete words without a mapping procedure. The intended method likely involves copying pre-trained word embeddings of the most frequent tokens. However, it’s unclear how repetitions are handled if the prefix length (200) doesn’t evenly divide the number of frequent words. Specify the initialization: e.g., 'The prefix embeddings are initialized by copying the pre-trained input embeddings of the most frequent words, repeated cyclically to fill the 200-token sequence.'

comments.title: Inconsistent description of lightweight tuning
comments.quote: lightweight fine-tuning, which freezes most of the model’s parameters, allowing a tuning of the token embeddings and softmax layer or the final layer alone
comments.explanation: The phrase 'token embeddings and softmax layer or the final layer alone' is ambiguous: it suggests a single method that either tunes input/output layers or the final layer, but the paper evaluates two distinct strategies—L-EO (embeddings and output) and L-LDB (last decoder block). This wording implies they are variants of the same approach, which misrepresents the experimental design. Rewrite to clearly distinguish the two: e.g., 'lightweight fine-tuning, which restricts updates to specific subsets such as token embeddings and output layers (L-EO) or the final decoder block (L-LDB)'.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper presents an empirical evaluation of four fine-tuning strategies for personalizing code generation models to specific Java projects. While the experimental setup is comprehensive and the results show consistent improvements across all personalization methods, there are significant concerns about the evaluation methodology, particularly the reliance on intrinsic metrics that may not reflect actual code quality, and the lack of proper statistical analysis for task-specific metrics. The paper also makes assumptions about generalizability without sufficient evidence.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by systematically comparing different personalization strategies for code generation models across multiple dimensions: performance, cost, and deployment considerations. The experimental design is thorough with 320 fine-tuned models across 20 projects using 4-fold cross-validation. The results clearly demonstrate that personalization provides significant benefits over the baseline model. However, the evaluation methodology has critical flaws that undermine confidence in the claimed improvements in actual code generation quality.

Overview issues:

- Inadequate validation of intrinsic metrics for code quality: The paper relies heavily on BLEU and perplexity scores as primary evaluation metrics (RQ1), but these intrinsic metrics have well-known limitations for code generation tasks. BLEU measures n-gram overlap which may not correlate with functional correctness, and perplexity only measures likelihood under the model's distribution. The paper shows large improvements in BLEU (from 16.1 to ~38) but provides no evidence that this translates to better code. This is particularly problematic because the task-specific metrics (perfect matches) show much more modest improvements (4-6% top-5). The authors should validate the correlation between BLEU/perplexity and actual code functionality, or at minimum acknowledge this limitation more strongly in their framing of results.

- Missing statistical significance testing for task-specific metrics: While the paper performs Kruskal-Wallis tests for intrinsic metrics (Table 4), it completely lacks statistical analysis for the more important task-specific metrics shown in Figure 3. The perfect match rates (4-6% vs <1% baseline) and abstracted matches (~13-14% vs 3%) represent the paper's strongest evidence of practical improvement, yet we have no indication whether these differences are statistically significant across projects. Given the variability in project characteristics (Table 2), proper significance testing is essential. The authors should report p-values or confidence intervals for these metrics to support their claims of significant improvements.

- Unfair comparison in prefix tuning initialization: The prefix tuning approach is disadvantaged by poor initialization (Section 4.3), requiring significantly more compute to achieve comparable performance. The authors acknowledge this but still present prefix tuning as a viable alternative without properly addressing this limitation. For a fair comparison, they should either: (1) use the initialization strategy they mention (training on a large generic dataset first), or (2) explicitly frame prefix tuning as having higher initialization costs that may be amortized over many users. As presented, the comparison unfairly penalizes prefix tuning and could mislead readers about its practical viability.

- Limited generalizability evidence beyond Java unit tests: The paper evaluates personalization exclusively on Java unit test generation using projects sampled from a single dataset. While the results are promising for this specific task, the authors make broad claims about personalization benefits for code generation in general without evidence. The introduction suggests applicability to "proprietary dependencies and code styles" more broadly, but the evaluation only covers one programming language and task type. The paper should either constrain its claims to the evaluated scenario or provide evidence (e.g., additional experiments) that the findings generalize to other languages or code generation tasks.

- Insufficient analysis of training cost fairness: The compute cost analysis (RQ3) uses an approximate formula (C≈2N) that may not accurately reflect real-world training costs, especially for approaches with different memory access patterns. More importantly, the paper doesn't account for potential differences in convergence rates or hyperparameter sensitivity across methods. For example, L-EO requires 2-3x more compute than Custom to reach similar validation loss, but this doesn't factor into the overall cost comparison. A more comprehensive analysis would measure actual wall-clock time and energy consumption on identical hardware, providing practitioners with actionable deployment guidance.

- Missing demonstration of coding style adaptation: The paper claims that custom models adapt to the coding style of the target project, but provides only weak evidence through tf-idf cosine similarity of identifiers. This metric doesn't capture important aspects of coding style like naming conventions, code structure, or design patterns. The paper should include concrete examples showing before/after comparisons of generated code from baseline vs. custom models, demonstrating how the custom model learns project-specific patterns. For instance, show how a custom model learns to use project-specific helper methods or follows the project's testing conventions that the baseline model ignores.

- No worked example of personalization benefits: The paper lacks a detailed case study walking through the personalization process for a specific project. Readers would benefit from seeing how the model improves on particular types of methods after personalization - perhaps methods using project-specific libraries or following unique patterns. The authors should select one representative project from Table 2 and show: (1) examples where baseline fails but custom model succeeds, (2) analysis of what types of code patterns the model learned, and (3) quantitative breakdown of improvements by code complexity or method type. This would make the abstract performance gains more concrete.

- Missing deployment scenario analysis: The paper claims to provide guidelines for choosing customization strategies based on deployment scenarios, but never actually models or analyzes these scenarios. The discussion mentions serving tens of thousands of models but provides no calculations of storage, memory, or serving costs. The authors should include a concrete analysis: for example, calculate the storage requirements for 10,000 custom models under each approach, estimate GPU memory needs for batch inference with prefix tuning, or model the cost trade-offs for different user counts. Without this, the deployment guidance remains speculative rather than actionable.

- No comparison with non-personalized baselines: The paper compares customization strategies against each other and a single baseline model, but doesn't explore whether simpler alternatives could achieve similar benefits. For example, could prompt engineering or few-shot learning with the base model achieve comparable performance without fine-tuning? The authors should include experiments testing: (1) in-context learning with project-specific examples in the prompt, (2) retrieval-augmented generation using project code, or (3) simple fine-tuning on a small subset of project data. This would establish whether full personalization is necessary or if lighter alternatives suffice.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add statistical significance testing for task-specific metrics (perfect matches and abstracted matches) with proper correction for multiple comparisons

- Validate the correlation between intrinsic metrics (BLEU/perplexity) and actual code functionality, or explicitly frame these as preliminary indicators only

- Implement fair initialization for prefix tuning or reframe the comparison to account for its higher initialization costs

- Constrain generalization claims to the evaluated scenario (Java unit tests) or provide additional evidence across different programming languages/tasks

- Provide more comprehensive cost analysis including wall-clock time and energy consumption on identical hardware

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in evaluation function definition
comments.quote: The performance of a machine learning model $m$ on a dataset $p$ is measured by one or more evaluation functions $f(m,p)$, where $f$ can be either a maximization (e.g., BLEU, top-k accuracy) or minimization (e.g., perplexity) function. The customization process is designed to modify the trainable parameters of the model $m$, obtaining the model $m^{\prime}$, such that the performance of $m^{\prime}$ on $p$ is better than what was attained by $m$. Specifically, $f(m^{\prime},p)>f(m,p)$ for maximization functions, or $f(m^{\prime},p)<f(m,p)$ for minimization functions.
comments.explanation: The text introduces a logical inconsistency by stating performance is measured by 'one or more evaluation functions' but then formalizes improvement using a single function $f$. When multiple evaluation functions exist, the comparison $f(m',p) > f(m,p)$ becomes mathematically ambiguous since different functions may give conflicting orderings. For example, a model might improve BLEU score while worsening perplexity. Rewrite the formalization as: 'Let $\mathcal{F} = \{f_1, f_2, ..., f_k\}$ be the set of evaluation functions, where each $f_i$ is either maximized or minimized. The customization process aims to obtain $m'$ such that for all $f_i \in \mathcal{F}$, the performance improves according to each function's optimization direction.'

comments.title: Insufficient evidence for L-LDB hypothesis
comments.quote: We observe that, as we go through the transformer model, the average change in parameter values tends to increase, with the last decoder block showing the highest changes in parameter values. As a result, we hypothesize that it could be sufficient to tune the last decoder block and obtain performance improvements similar to the fully custom fine-tuned model.
comments.explanation: The logical leap from correlation to causation is problematic. The authors observe that the last decoder block exhibits the highest parameter changes during full fine-tuning, then conclude that tuning only this block should achieve similar performance. However, this reasoning assumes that the last block's changes are independent of updates in earlier blocks, which may not be true. The last block could be changing more precisely because it's responding to and building upon representations learned in earlier layers. Rewrite the conclusion as 'As a result, we hypothesize that it could be sufficient to tune the last decoder block and obtain performance improvements similar to the fully custom fine-tuned model, and we will empirically validate this hypothesis in our experiments.'

comments.title: Parameter count calculation needs justification
comments.quote: Practically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of  $1024 \times 200 \times 24 \times 2 \approx 10\mathrm{M}$  trainable parameters. The prefix is initialized to the most frequent words in the repository for which the model is customized.
comments.explanation: The calculation $1024 \times 200 \times 24 \times 2 = 9,830,400$ rounds to approximately 9.8M, not 10M. More importantly, the multiplication by 24 and 2 lacks justification in the text. The paper should explicitly state that the model has 24 layers and that prefixes are applied to both encoder and decoder blocks. Rewrite the calculation as $1024 \times 200 \times L \times B$ where L=24 layers and B=2 blocks (encoder/decoder), with these values explicitly justified by referencing the model architecture.

comments.title: Compute cost approximation lacks justification
comments.quote: For a model with $N$ parameters, we approximate the computation cost of a forward pass to be $C\approx 2N$ floating point operations per training token, with an additional correction for embedding layers. The backward pass takes roughly twice the amount of compute, but it is unnecessary for layers that are frozen.
comments.explanation: The $C\approx 2N$ FLOPs per token formula for forward pass is not justified. For transformer models, forward pass FLOPs depend on sequence length $L$, hidden dimension $d$, and number of layers $h$. The dominant cost comes from attention matrices ($O(L^2d)$) and feed-forward layers ($O(Ld^2)$), which cannot be reduced to a simple function of total parameters $N$ alone. The reference to Kaplan et al. (2020) Table 1 is inappropriate as it discusses scaling laws, not per-token FLOP counts. Rewrite the compute approximation as $C_{forward} \approx 2 \times (\text{attention FLOPs} + \text{FFN FLOPs})$ with explicit dependence on architectural hyperparameters, or provide a citation that specifically derives the $2N$ approximation for transformers.

comments.title: Embedding layer correction unspecified
comments.quote: For a model with $N$ parameters, we approximate the computation cost of a forward pass to be $C\approx 2N$ floating point operations per training token, with an additional correction for embedding layers.
comments.explanation: The 'additional correction for embedding layers' is never defined, making the cost comparison incomplete. Embedding layers involve lookup operations with FLOP count $O(L \times d_{embed})$ per token, different from dense layer matrix multiplications. Without specifying this correction term, comparisons between methods that tune embeddings (L-EO) versus those that don't (L-LDB) cannot be properly evaluated. Add the specific correction formula: 'with an additional correction of $L \times d_{embed}$ FLOPs per token for embedding lookups' or provide the exact correction term used in the analysis.

comments.title: Contradictory performance ranking claims
comments.quote: Between the different customization processes, Custom consistently performs the best, closely followed by Prefix and L-LDB. When considering abstracted code matches, these three approaches are nearly identical. L-EO, however, performs slightly worse than the others.
comments.explanation: The text claims Custom consistently performs best followed by Prefix and L-LDB, with L-EO performing slightly worse. However, Table 4 shows p-values of 0.4-0.9 between all customization methods, indicating no statistically significant differences. The p-value between Custom and L-EO is 0.4 for BLEU4 and 0.5 for perplexity, contradicting the claim that L-EO performs 'slightly worse'. Similarly, the p-value between Custom and Prefix is 0.7 for both metrics, contradicting the claim that Custom 'consistently performs the best'. Rewrite the performance comparison to reflect that while all customization methods significantly outperform baseline, there are no statistically significant differences between the customization methods themselves.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in first epoch gains claim
comments.quote: Custom achieves very large gains during the first epoch, as evidenced by the fact that its validation loss starts much lower than L-EO and L-LDB.
comments.explanation: The reasoning contains a logical contradiction. The evidence cited (lower starting validation loss) supports better initial performance, not large gains during the first epoch. First epoch gains would be demonstrated by the rate of loss decrease from the starting point, not the starting point itself. Rewrite this as 'Custom achieves better initial performance, as evidenced by the fact that its validation loss starts much lower than L-EO and L-LDB.'

comments.title: Overstated claim about significant improvements
comments.quote: In our extensive empirical evaluation we found that all the customization strategies lead to significant model’s improvements on a target project, in terms of both intrinsic and task-specific metrics, with the custom models adapting to the coding style of the target project.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims all customization strategies lead to 'significant' improvements, but the paper's own results show this is not uniformly true. For task-specific metrics, Figure 3 shows perfect match rates of only 4-6% for custom strategies versus <1% for baseline - while this is an improvement, the absolute performance remains very low. More importantly, the paper lacks statistical significance testing for these task-specific metrics (unlike the intrinsic metrics in Table 4), so we cannot conclude these improvements are statistically significant. Rewrite as 'In our extensive empirical evaluation we found that all the customization strategies lead to improvements on a target project across both intrinsic and task-specific metrics, with intrinsic improvements showing statistical significance' because the task-specific improvements lack statistical validation and absolute performance remains modest.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about coding style adaptation
comments.quote: with the custom models adapting to the coding style of the target project.
comments.explanation: The conclusion states that custom models adapt to the coding style of the target project, but the paper provides only weak evidence for this claim. Section 4.2 mentions using tf-idf cosine similarity of identifiers to show custom models use variable and function names more similar to developers' own tests. However, this metric only captures identifier similarity and doesn't demonstrate broader coding style adaptation (e.g., code structure, design patterns, testing conventions). Without concrete examples showing how custom models learn project-specific patterns beyond just identifier names, this claim overstates what was actually demonstrated. Remove 'with the custom models adapting to the coding style of the target project' because the paper only provides limited evidence (identifier similarity) for coding style adaptation, not comprehensive demonstration of style learning.

comments.title: Missing definition of parameter change metric
comments.quote: Figure 2 reports the average absolute changes, during fine-tuning, in the parameters belonging to different Encoder and Decoder blocks for a BART model.
comments.explanation: The text mentions 'average absolute changes' in parameters but does not specify how this metric is computed. Is it the L1 norm of weight differences? Is it averaged per parameter, per layer, or per block? Without a clear definition, the observation about increasing parameter changes cannot be properly interpreted or reproduced. Add a sentence specifying the exact metric: 'We compute the average absolute change as the mean of |θ_fine-tuned - θ_pre-trained| across all parameters in each block.'

comments.title: Prefix initialization method unclear
comments.quote: The prefix is initialized to the most frequent words in the repository for which the model is customized.
comments.explanation: The initialization method is ambiguous. Are the virtual tokens initialized to the embeddings of the most frequent words, or are the actual word tokens used? If embeddings are used, how are the 200 token positions filled when there may be fewer than 200 frequent words? Add clarification: 'The prefix parameters are initialized to the embedding vectors of the top 200 most frequent words in the repository, with zero padding if fewer than 200 unique words exist.'

comments.title: Undefined compute metric PF-seconds
comments.quote: Figure 4: Validation Loss vs Compute (PF-seconds) - Light lines represent the validation loss curve for each individual project and fold, while the bold line represents the average for each custom strategy.
comments.explanation: The compute metric 'PF-seconds' is used in Figure 4 but never defined in the text. Without defining what PF-seconds measures (e.g., petaflop-seconds, processor-seconds, or some other unit), readers cannot interpret the absolute scale of compute costs or verify the quantitative claims. Add a definition of PF-seconds in section 3.4 or in the figure caption.

comments.title: Incorrect model size claim
comments.quote: At the time of writing such large models cannot be executed in a reasonable amount of time on most consumer laptops.
comments.explanation: The claim that 10-100 million parameter models cannot be executed reasonably on consumer laptops is inaccurate. Models in this range (like GPT-2 small with 117M parameters) run comfortably on modern consumer laptops with CPUs and even faster with consumer GPUs. The paper's own experiments likely used models in this range, contradicting this claim. This statement should be corrected to reflect actual computational requirements or specify the exact model sizes used.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper investigates four strategies for fine-tuning Transformer-based code generation models to specific software projects, evaluating them on the task of generating unit tests for Java methods.

The paper provides a clear and well-structured empirical study on parameter-efficient fine-tuning for code generation. While the results are promising, the work lacks a rigorous discussion of potential data leakage and the correlation between the chosen intrinsic metrics and actual developer utility.

Overview issues:

- Conflation of Intrinsic Metrics with Functional Correctness: The authors rely heavily on BLEU scores and perplexity to evaluate the efficacy of their personalized models (Section 3.2, 4.1). In the context of code generation, these metrics are notoriously poor proxies for functional correctness or developer productivity. A model might achieve high BLEU scores by matching variable names or boilerplate code while failing to produce syntactically valid or logically sound unit tests. The paper should include a more rigorous evaluation of functional correctness, such as passing the generated tests against the focal method, rather than relying on string-matching metrics.

- Lack of Data Leakage Analysis: The methodology involves fine-tuning models on a specific project and evaluating them on the same project (Section 3). Given that codebases often contain repetitive patterns, utility functions, and specific naming conventions, there is a high risk that the model is simply memorizing project-specific identifiers rather than learning to generalize coding intent. The paper does not discuss or control for the possibility that the test set contains code snippets highly similar to the training set. A cross-project evaluation or a de-duplication analysis is necessary to ensure the improvements are not merely artifacts of training on test data.

- Inadequate Baseline Comparison: The paper compares custom models against a single baseline (AthenaTest) but does not explore how these personalization techniques compare to simpler, non-neural heuristics or retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches. If the goal is to improve performance on private repositories, it is unclear if fine-tuning is more cost-effective than a RAG-based system that retrieves relevant code snippets from the target project. The absence of a comparison against a retrieval-based baseline makes it difficult to judge whether the computational overhead of fine-tuning is justified.

- Unclear Generalizability Across Languages and Contexts: The entire study is restricted to Java unit test generation (Section 3). Programming languages vary significantly in syntax, type systems, and idiomatic patterns, which may impact how successfully a model can be personalized via prefix tuning or last-layer adaptation. The authors should at least acknowledge this limitation or provide a brief analysis of how their findings might differ for languages with different characteristics, such as Python or C++, to support their broader claims about personalization.

- Missing validation of functional correctness via execution: The paper relies on BLEU, perplexity, and string-matching metrics like 'perfect match' to evaluate unit test generation. These metrics do not guarantee that the generated tests are executable or actually test the focal method correctly. To demonstrate the practical utility of the proposed customization strategies, the authors should execute the generated unit tests against the target methods and report the Pass@k metric. This would provide a necessary check on whether the model is learning functional logic or merely mimicking surface-level syntax.

- Absence of a concrete comparison with retrieval-based baselines: The authors argue that customization is necessary for adapting to proprietary dependencies and coding styles. However, they do not compare their fine-tuning methods against a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) baseline, which is a standard and often more cost-effective approach for incorporating project-specific context. Without such a comparison, it is impossible to determine if the computational and storage overhead of maintaining personalized models is justified. The paper should include a RAG baseline to provide a clearer picture of the trade-offs in performance and deployment cost.

- Lack of evidence for cross-project generalization: The paper evaluates personalization by training and testing on the same project, which risks overfitting to project-specific memorization rather than learning a transferable 'coding style'. To demonstrate that the models are learning generalizable patterns, the authors should perform a cross-project evaluation where a model customized on one set of projects is tested on a held-out project. This would verify whether the customization strategies actually capture stylistic features that are useful beyond the specific training data provided.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper presents a solid empirical foundation, but the reliance on intrinsic metrics and the lack of functional validation prevent it from making a definitive contribution to the field of code generation. The authors must demonstrate that their personalized models produce functional code and address the risks of data leakage.

Revision targets:

- Perform a functional evaluation (e.g., compile and run generated tests) to determine if the models produce executable code.

- Conduct a de-duplication analysis or a cross-project evaluation to ensure that the performance gains are not due to memorization of the test set.

- Add a discussion on the correlation between the reported BLEU/perplexity improvements and actual developer productivity or functional correctness.

- Compare the fine-tuning results against a simple retrieval-based baseline to justify the computational cost of the proposed methods.

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of LLM parameter counts
comments.quote: This paper does not evaluate methods for client side training with restricted resources, however, as the most powerful large language models which enable state of the art code synthesis have 10-100 million parameters.
comments.explanation: The claim that state-of-the-art code synthesis models have only 10-100 million parameters is incorrect. By the time of the cited works (e.g., Austin et al. 2021, Chen et al. 2021), models like Codex were already in the billion-parameter range. This significantly underestimates the scale of modern models and misrepresents the computational landscape.

comments.title: Inappropriate use of Kruskal-Wallis test for pairwise comparisons
comments.quote: Specifically, we rely on the Kruskal-Wallis test, a non-parametric statistical test.
comments.explanation: The Kruskal-Wallis test is an omnibus test for determining if at least one group in a set differs from the others; it is not designed for pairwise comparisons. Using it to generate a matrix of pairwise p-values, as shown in Table 4, is statistically invalid. Please replace this with a post-hoc procedure (e.g., Dunn's test) or pairwise tests (e.g., Mann-Whitney U) with appropriate multiple-comparison corrections.

comments.title: Ambiguity in parameter change metric
comments.quote: Figure 2 reports the average absolute changes, during fine-tuning, in the parameters belonging to different Encoder and Decoder blocks for a BART model.
comments.explanation: Using 'average absolute change' to compare layers is problematic because Transformer layers have heterogeneous architectures. Feedforward layers contain significantly more parameters than attention projections, and LayerNorm parameters have different scales. This metric is likely dominated by layer size rather than functional adaptation. Please demonstrate that the observation holds under a normalized metric, such as the Frobenius norm of the update relative to the initial weights.

comments.title: Inaccurate scaling of training cost with trainable parameters
comments.quote: The backward pass takes roughly twice the amount of compute, but it is unnecessary for layers that are frozen.
comments.explanation: The cost approximation $C \approx 2N$ fails to distinguish between total parameters $N$ and trainable parameters $N_{trainable}$. In parameter-efficient tuning, the backward pass cost is dominated by the gradient computation for $N_{trainable}$ parameters, not the total model size $N$. By using $N$ as the multiplier, the paper overestimates the cost of parameter-efficient methods. Please revise the cost estimation to reflect that the backward pass cost is proportional to $N_{trainable}$.

comments.title: Ambiguity in model architecture description
comments.quote: Figure 1(a) shows the model during fine-tuning, where all the parameters from the encoder and decoder blocks, as well as embeddings and output layers can be modified.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'encoder and decoder blocks,' which implies an encoder-decoder architecture. However, code generation is often performed by decoder-only models. If the model is decoder-only, referring to 'encoder blocks' is misleading. Please clarify the architecture or remove the reference to encoder blocks if the model is decoder-only.

comments.title: Inconsistent denominator for percentage calculation
comments.quote:
Finally, Prefix tuning has the lowest number of trainable parameters, only  $2.4\%$  (10M) of the total, but these are additional parameters added to the model, which reaches a total of 416M.

|  Customi
comments.explanation: The percentage of trainable parameters is calculated inconsistently. For Custom, L-EO, and L-LDB, the percentage is relative to the 406M base model, but for Prefix tuning, it is relative to the 416M augmented total. Standardize the denominator to the base model size (406M) for all methods to ensure a consistent comparison.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper empirically compares four fine-tuning strategies for personalizing a BART-based code generation model on Java unit test generation. It evaluates intrinsic metrics, task-specific string matches, and theoretical compute costs across 20 GitHub projects. The findings suggest that all customization methods improve upon the baseline, with full fine-tuning offering the best performance and prefix tuning requiring the most compute due to initialization issues.

The work addresses a practical problem in deploying personalized code models and provides a clear taxonomy of parameter-efficient strategies. The experimental setup is straightforward, and the parameter counting analysis offers useful baseline insights. However, the evaluation lacks execution-based validation, the statistical methodology contains a fundamental misapplication, and the compute cost estimation ignores real-world hardware bottlenecks. These gaps limit the paper’s impact on both academic and engineering audiences.

Overview issues:

- Execution and compilation metrics are absent from the evaluation framework: The paper evaluates unit test generation using BLEU, perplexity, and string matches (Section 3.3, Table 3, Figure 3). For code synthesis, these intrinsic metrics correlate poorly with functional correctness. A model could output syntactically plausible but semantically broken tests that never compile. The authors should incorporate execution-based metrics like compilation success rates or pass@k. Adding a runtime evaluation step would ground the findings in actual software engineering utility and clarify whether the observed BLEU gains translate to deployable test cases.

- Statistical testing methodology misapplies Kruskal-Wallis for pairwise comparisons: Section 3.2 states the authors rely on the Kruskal-Wallis test to investigate differences between customization approaches. Kruskal-Wallis is a non-parametric alternative to one-way ANOVA and tests for differences across three or more independent groups simultaneously. It does not yield pairwise p-values. Table 4 reports pairwise p-values ranging from 0.4 to 0.9, which suggests either a different test was run but mislabeled, or the global test was incorrectly interpreted. This undermines the statistical rigor of the claims in Section 4.1. The authors should switch to Mann-Whitney U tests or Wilcoxon signed-rank tests for pairwise comparisons, apply a multiple testing correction like Bonferroni, and report exact test statistics alongside p-values.
